12. Appendix: ADR Index

- ADR-001: Use Rust for the local CLI implementation.
- ADR-002: Use SQLite as the single metadata and search engine with FTS5/BM25.
- ADR-003: Implement exhaustive Gemini chunk review as an explicit robust mode and correctness oracle.
- ADR-004: Implement a fast lexical retrieval path using multi-granularity indexing and RRF fusion.
- ADR-005: Use a corpus-aware Gemini planner plus a deterministic rules-fast planner.
- ADR-006: Add session-derived entity extraction before repository-wide code parsing.
- ADR-007: Use preset-driven CLI commands rather than a flag-heavy primary interface.

13. Consistency Check

- RTM covers REQ-001 through REQ-018.
- Every phase P00 through P06 has populated metrics.
- Every phase includes explicit RED, GREEN, REFACTOR, and MEASURE steps with verification commands.
- Every TEST-### includes a concrete file path and exact command.
- Every acceptance criterion is supported by at least one REQ and one test path.

Check the plan. We don't want to overengineer things; we want one way of
doing things, meaning no legacy and no fallbacks. Prefer direct approaches
over adapters and safety for situations that are unlikely to happen. Prefer
general code over minor performance gains because we want the codebase to be
smaller.

Findings:

- High: the plan still commits to multiple ways of doing the same job: two retrieval systems, two planners, and an explicit fallback/oracle story. That conflicts directly with your “one way, no fallbacks” rule. See [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:23](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:23), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:30](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:30), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:109](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:109), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:113](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:113), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:352](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:352), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:388](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:388), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:426](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:426).

- High: the plan adds defensive architecture for unlikely failures: versioned parser adapters, strict schema/retry layers, deterministic fallback planner, and mock/live Gemini boundaries. That is safety-heavy engineering for a small local CLI. See [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:257](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:257), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:261](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:261), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:329](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:329), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:365](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:365).

- High: the data model is too normalized for the likely first useful version. `source_sessions`, `turn_groups`, `chunk_windows`, `entities`, `entity_mentions`, `vocab_stats`, `retrieval_runs`, `retrieval_results`, and two FTS tables are a lot of moving pieces before the first search command exists. See [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:790](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:790), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:801](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:801), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:810](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:810), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:829](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:829), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:834](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:834), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:848](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:848).

- Medium: the process plan is heavier than the codebase warrants right now. A full evaluation harness, three eval tiers, sixteen named tests, RTM coverage, and current-session oracle calibration are strong for a mature system, but heavy for an empty repo where the first milestone is “can it find the right thread with citations.” See [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:41](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:41), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:65](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:65), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:498](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:498), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:534](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:534), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:617](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:617).

- Medium: the product surface is broader than necessary. Four presets, multi-depth modes, handoff output, machine-readable contracts, exhaustive mode, and lexical mode create a larger interface before the core search behavior is proven. See [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:105](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:105), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:107](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:107), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:119](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:119), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:136](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:136), [ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:462](/home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md:462).

Recommended simplification:

- Pick one retrieval path. My recommendation is: `filter local corpus -> build one query plan -> search one SQLite FTS index -> rank with one scoring formula -> return cited snippets`. No separate exhaustive mode, no lexical-vs-Gemini split, no fallback planner.

- Collapse the data model to a smaller core: `sessions`, `chunks`, one FTS table, and maybe one simple `entities` table if exact identifiers materially help. Drop retrieval-run telemetry tables and vocab tables until they are proven necessary.

- Reduce the plan to a smaller delivery shape: `P00 bootstrap`, `P01 ingest + chunk`, `P02 query builder + search`, `P03 CLI output + a handful of gold tests`. Keep maybe 4-6 essential tests, with the current-session regression as the main anchor.

If you want, I can rewrite the plan file to match this stricter “single direct path” philosophy.

=== document: plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md ===
1. Title and Metadata

- Project name: Ask Codex Session Search CLI
- Version: 0.1.0-plan
- Owners: Repository maintainer; implementation owner to be assigned in repo
- Date: 2026-03-06
- Document ID: PLAN-ASKCODEX-2026-03-06-001
- Summary:
  - Build a local-first Rust CLI that searches prior Codex CLI sessions stored on the developer workstation, retrieves the most relevant discussions for a natural-language query, and returns cited verbatim evidence. The current repository is nearly empty and contains no application code, no Cargo project, and no tests; the executable system described here will be introduced under `src/`, `tests/`, and `plans/` while reading session data from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`.

2. Design Consensus & Trade-offs

- Topic: Implementation language
  - Verdict: FOR Rust
  - Rationale: The system needs a fast local CLI, safe parsing of large local corpora, good SQLite support, and low-memory execution without the complexity of C++.
- Topic: Storage and search engine
  - Verdict: FOR SQLite as the single local engine
  - Rationale: SQLite already exists in local Codex state, supports metadata filtering and FTS5/BM25, and avoids operational overhead from a second search service.
- Topic: Dense vector retrieval
  - Verdict: AGAINST for the first implementation
  - Rationale: The corpus is local, technical, metadata-rich, and exact identifiers matter; lexical retrieval plus citations is a better first fit than embeddings.
- Topic: Retrieval architecture
  - Verdict: FOR dual-mode retrieval
  - Rationale: The system needs an expensive but robust Gemini chunk-review mode and a faster multi-lane lexical path; both modes are required.
- Topic: Build order for retrieval
  - Verdict: FOR exhaustive Gemini mode before the fast lexical optimizer
  - Rationale: The exhaustive mode provides a correctness-oriented oracle and fallback path before introducing fast lexical scoring and fusion.
- Topic: Query planning
  - Verdict: FOR two-phase corpus-aware planning plus a rules-fast planner
  - Rationale: Gemini-based query planning should be constrained by terms that actually occur in the filtered corpus, while a non-LLM fast path is needed for low-latency usage.
- Topic: Domain expertise
  - Verdict: FOR session-derived entity extraction before repository parsing
  - Rationale: File paths, identifiers, config keys, commands, and symbols appearing in sessions are high-signal and cheaper to extract than parsing every repository file.
- Topic: Fusion
  - Verdict: FOR Reciprocal Rank Fusion
  - Rationale: Multiple lexical lanes and query variants will produce heterogeneous rankings; RRF is robust without per-lane score normalization.
- Topic: Product surface
  - Verdict: FOR preset-driven CLI
  - Rationale: The tool should be easy to start with and should not require many flags for common tasks like latest-spec search or exhaustive review.
- Topic: Testing strategy
  - Verdict: FOR a full evaluation harness
  - Rationale: Retrieval quality must be measured against real golden cases, including the current conversation thread `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443`.
- Topic: Session depth
  - Verdict: FOR indexing dialogue, tools, edits, and reasoning-aware depth controls
  - Rationale: Default output should stay dialogue-first, but the system must retain richer content for explicit deeper modes.

3. PRD (IEEE 29148 Stakeholder/System Needs)

- Problem
  - Prior Codex CLI sessions contain design history, implementation rationale, exact identifiers, and decisions that are difficult to rediscover manually.
  - Local session data exists but is split across a SQLite metadata store and rollout JSONL files, making ad hoc recovery slow and error-prone.
- Users
  - Primary user: a developer using Codex CLI who wants to recover previous design discussions, latest specs, historical decisions, and exact cited context.
  - Secondary user: an AI assistant operating in the same workspace that needs reliable local memory retrieval with citations.
- Value
  - Reduce repeated design work.
  - Recover exact prior decisions and identifiers.
  - Provide cited evidence for past discussions rather than paraphrased guesses.
- Business Goals
  - Deliver a working local CLI with trustworthy citations.
  - Minimize operational complexity by keeping all retrieval local.
  - Enable a fast preset path and a robust exhaustive mode.
- Success Metrics
  - Gold-query Recall@5 on curated fixtures >= 0.90.
  - Current-session regression Top-1 exact thread hit = 1.00.
  - Citation completeness = 100% for returned findings.
  - Fast preset latency p95 <= 2.0 s on a local corpus of up to 1,000 threads.
  - Exhaustive preset latency p95 <= 90 s on filtered corpora up to 200 chunks.
  - Dialogue-only depth leakage of tool payloads = 0 in gold tests.
- Scope
  - Local CLI only.
  - Read-only access to local Codex session stores.
  - Search modes: exhaustive, lexical, hybrid fusion.
  - Presets, query planning, citations, handoff output, and evaluation harness.
- Non-goals
  - Web UI.
  - Cloud-hosted search services.
  - Embedding/vector search in the first release.
  - Automatic modification of past sessions.
- Dependencies
  - Rust stable toolchain.
  - Gemini CLI installed and authenticated locally.
  - Local Codex data present at `/home/kirill/.codex/`.
  - SQLite with FTS5 support available via Rust dependencies.
- Risks
  - Codex storage formats may change over time.
  - Gemini CLI output may vary and require strict schema enforcement.
  - Local corpus size and repeated boilerplate may reduce retrieval quality if not normalized.
- Assumptions
  - The repository remains local-first.
  - The user wants both robust and fast modes.
  - The current repository state is authoritative: root contains `.gitignore`, `LICENSE`, and `firebase-debug.log`, and no application code exists yet.

4. SRS (IEEE 29148 Canonical Requirements)

4.1 Functional Requirements

- REQ-001, type: func
  - The system shall ingest Codex thread metadata from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and rollout files from `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`.
- REQ-002, type: func
  - The system shall filter candidate sessions by repo/cwd, timeframe, and optional branch or origin URL before retrieval.
- REQ-003, type: func
  - The system shall normalize source data into session records, turn groups, chunk windows, and entity mentions.
- REQ-004, type: func
  - The system shall support depth-aware retrieval modes: `dialogue`, `tools`, `edits`, `reasoning`, and `full`.
- REQ-005, type: func
  - The system shall expose preset-driven CLI entry points for at least `latest-spec`, `design-history`, `fast-find`, and `exhaustive-audit`.
- REQ-006, type: func
  - The system shall provide an exhaustive Gemini chunk-review mode that scans filtered chunks in parallel and returns cited findings.
- REQ-007, type: func
  - The system shall provide a lexical retrieval path using FTS5/BM25, exact phrase matching, identifier/path matching, and optional regex matching.
- REQ-008, type: func
  - The system shall provide two query planners: a two-phase corpus-aware Gemini planner and a deterministic rules-fast planner.
- REQ-009, type: func
  - The system shall fuse multi-query and multi-lane retrieval results using RRF with recency and exact-match boosts.
- REQ-010, type: func
  - The system shall return verbatim evidence with thread ID, date, rollout path, and cited snippet for every final finding.
- REQ-011, type: func
  - The system shall emit a current-session handoff block suitable for pasting into an active AI session.
- REQ-012, type: func
  - The system shall include an evaluation harness with golden retrieval cases, including the current conversation session `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443`.

4.2 Non-functional Requirements

- REQ-013, type: nfr
  - The system shall remain local-first and shall not require a remote database or search service.
- REQ-014, type: perf
  - The system shall support incremental indexing and avoid full rescans when source threads are unchanged.
- REQ-015, type: nfr
  - The system shall provide deterministic test behavior through fixed seeds, mock transports, and reproducible fixtures.

4.3 Interfaces/APIs

- REQ-016, type: int
  - The CLI contract shall support preset-oriented commands and machine-readable output.
  - Planned commands:
    - `ask-codex-sessions latest-spec "<query>"`
    - `ask-codex-sessions design-history "<query>"`
    - `ask-codex-sessions fast-find "<query>"`
    - `ask-codex-sessions exhaustive-audit "<query>"`
  - Planned core Rust types:
    - `ThreadMeta`
    - `TurnGroup`
    - `ChunkWindow`
    - `Entity`
    - `QueryPlan`
    - `PlannerMode`
    - `PresetProfile`
    - `RetrievalLane`
    - `RetrievalResult`
    - `Citation`

4.4 Data Requirements

- REQ-017, type: data
  - The system shall maintain a normalized local index with explicit invariants for source timestamps, chunk boundaries, entity normalization, and depth separation.
  - The system shall preserve exact values for paths, identifiers, session IDs, and quoted evidence.
  - The system shall never include reasoning or tool payloads in `dialogue` mode output.

4.5 Error & Telemetry Expectations

- REQ-018, type: func
  - The system shall emit structured errors for missing local Codex sources, malformed rollout files, invalid Gemini responses, and citation assembly failures.
  - The system shall record retrieval-run telemetry sufficient to debug query plans, lane scores, and result fusion without storing private output beyond local disk.

4.6 Acceptance Criteria

- A query for the current project’s current session returns thread `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` with the correct rollout path and verbatim supporting text.
- `dialogue` mode returns only user/assistant conversation text.
- `exhaustive-audit` and `fast-find` both produce citations, but exhaustive mode may be slower.
- The corpus-aware planner does not introduce expansion terms absent from the filtered corpus.
- Presets run with sensible defaults and do not require the user to set many flags for common tasks.
- Incremental indexing updates only changed threads.

5. Iterative Implementation & Test Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 + 29119-3)

- Phase Strategy
  - Build a correctness-oriented exhaustive retrieval path first.
  - Use the exhaustive path as an oracle and fallback while introducing the faster lexical system.
  - Introduce multi-granularity indexing before retrieval optimization.
  - Keep phases atomic, test-first, and reversible.
- Compute Policy
  - branch_limits: 2 active implementation branches per phase maximum.
  - reflection_passes: 1 explicit reflection pass after first green per phase.
  - early_stop%: 90%; stop additional tuning in a phase once all exit gates are green and metrics meet threshold.
- Governance
  - Any threshold change requires a new ADR entry before implementation continues.
- State Safety
  - Create a git tag at each green phase boundary: `plan-p00-green`, `plan-p01-green`, and so on.
- Risk Register
  - Risk: Codex source format drift
    - Trigger: rollout parser fails on unseen event types
    - Mitigation: versioned parser adapters and ignored-unknown event handling

  - Risk: Gemini JSON contract instability
    - Trigger: planner or exhaustive mode receives invalid structured output
    - Mitigation: strict schema validation, retries, deterministic fallback planner
  - Risk: Lexical query drift from bad expansions
    - Trigger: recall drops after planner introduction
    - Mitigation: constrain expansions to observed corpus terms and keep the original query intact
  - Risk: Boilerplate dominates rankings
    - Trigger: system instructions outrank conversation content
    - Mitigation: normalization rules, dialogue-only fields, entity side index
  - Risk: Empty repo state delays testing
    - Trigger: no runner or fixtures exist
    - Mitigation: bootstrap Cargo and fixtures in P00
- Suspension/Resumption Criteria
  - Suspend if the parser cannot reliably ingest local Codex sources.
  - Suspend if TEST-015 fails after P03 because the current-session regression is the main truth anchor.
  - Resume only after the failing parser or retrieval regression is isolated with a deterministic fixture.

### Phase P06: Evaluation Harness, Incremental Indexing, and Hardening

A. Scope and Objectives

- Impacted REQ-012, REQ-014, REQ-015, REQ-018
- Finalize the evaluation harness, current-session regression, and incremental reindexing.

B. Iterative Execution Steps

- Step 1 (RED): Create/update TEST-014 in `tests/incremental_update.rs` for REQ-014 and REQ-018 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-014 cargo test test_incremental_update_skips_unchanged_threads --test incremental_update -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because source fingerprinting and incremental updates are absent.
- Step 2 (GREEN): Implement source fingerprinting and incremental reindex decisions in `src/index/incremental.rs` -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-014 cargo test test_incremental_update_skips_unchanged_threads --test incremental_update -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 3 (RED): Create/update TEST-015 in `tests/current_session_regression.rs` for REQ-001, REQ-002, REQ-005, REQ-006, REQ-007, REQ-009, REQ-010, and REQ-012 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-015 cargo test test_current_session_regression_finds_019cc49c_thread --test current_session_regression -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because full end-to-end quality and ranking are not yet locked.
- Step 4 (GREEN): Calibrate final fusion defaults, exhaustive preset behavior, and gold fixtures until regression passes -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-015 cargo test test_current_session_regression_finds_019cc49c_thread --test current_session_regression -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 5 (REFACTOR): Consolidate eval fixtures, reusable assertions, and telemetry summaries under `tests/support/` -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-015 cargo test test_current_session_regression_finds_019cc49c_thread --test current_session_regression -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 6 (MEASURE): Run the full quality gate -> run `EVAL_ID=EVAL-003 cargo test -- --nocapture` -> expected: thresholds met.

C. Exit Gate Rules

- Green: incremental indexing works and the current-session regression passes consistently.
- Yellow: indexing works but regression is flaky or latency thresholds are missed.
- Red: the system cannot reliably recover the current session and cite it.

8. Data Contract

- Schema snapshot
  - `source_sessions`
    - `thread_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY`
    - `created_at INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `updated_at INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `cwd TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `title TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `rollout_path TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `git_branch TEXT`
    - `git_origin_url TEXT`
    - `first_user_message TEXT`
  - `turn_groups`
    - `group_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY`
    - `thread_id TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `ordinal INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `user_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `assistant_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `dialogue_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `depth_mask INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `token_estimate INTEGER NOT NULL`
  - `chunk_windows`
    - `chunk_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY`
    - `thread_id TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `start_group INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `end_group INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `dialogue_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `tools_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `edits_text TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `reasoning_text TEXT NOT NULL`
  - `entities`
    - `entity_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`
    - `kind TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `value_exact TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `value_norm TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `doc_freq INTEGER NOT NULL`
  - `entity_mentions`
    - `chunk_id TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `entity_id INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `source_kind TEXT NOT NULL`
  - `vocab_stats`
    - `scope_hash TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `term TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `doc_freq INTEGER NOT NULL`
    - `term_freq INTEGER NOT NULL`
  - `retrieval_runs`
    - `run_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY`
    - `preset TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `planner_mode TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `retrieval_mode TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `query_json TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `created_at INTEGER NOT NULL`
  - `retrieval_results`
    - `run_id TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `chunk_id TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `lane TEXT NOT NULL`
    - `score_raw REAL NOT NULL`
    - `score_fused REAL NOT NULL`
    - `rank INTEGER NOT NULL`
  - `fts_dialogue`
    - FTS5 virtual table over `chunk_id` and `dialogue_text`
  - `fts_entities`
    - FTS5 virtual table over `chunk_id` and normalized entity text
- Invariants
  - Each `chunk_window` references one and only one `thread_id`.
  - `dialogue_text` must exclude tool and reasoning payload when depth is `dialogue`.
  - `value_exact` for entities is never lowercased or mutated.
  - `value_norm` may split camelCase, snake_case, kebab-case, and path segments.
  - Every citation must map to a valid `thread_id` and an existing source snippet boundary.

12. Appendix: ADR Index

- ADR-001: Use Rust for the local CLI implementation.
- ADR-002: Use SQLite as the single metadata and search engine with FTS5/BM25.
- ADR-003: Implement exhaustive Gemini chunk review as an explicit robust mode and correctness oracle.
- ADR-004: Implement a fast lexical retrieval path using multi-granularity indexing and RRF fusion.
- ADR-005: Use a corpus-aware Gemini planner plus a deterministic rules-fast planner.
- ADR-006: Add session-derived entity extraction before repository-wide code parsing.
- ADR-007: Use preset-driven CLI commands rather than a flag-heavy primary interface.

payload.text:
**Modifying Retrieval Plan**

I need to rethink the plan since the user wants to keep both LLM-based and hybrid retrieval. I'll probably rewrite the entire plan to simplify things while preserving the necessary wrapper. It seems I should focus on a single pipeline integrating Gemini query planning with hybrid retrieval. The outline would be filter, ingest/index, Gemini creating a structured multi-query plan, and running the hybrid retrieval process. I should avoid fallbacks and stick with the Gemini planner for clarity.

**Refining Retrieval Approach**

The user wants to maintain both LLM-based and hybrid retrieval without any fallbacks, so I can eliminate the rules-fast planner. I think using Gemini to build one query plan could work. I’ll structure it with a metadata filter and create a corpus summary from the filtered sessions, focusing on entity counts and terms. Then I'll ask Gemini to create a structured query plan and execute hybrid retrieval with the necessary lanes, ultimately fusing them using a scoring formula. However, I wonder if simplifying to one SQL query might be more effective.

**Simplifying Retrieval Framework**

I’m considering how hybrid retrieval could work. Maybe the simplest approach combines FTS/BM25 with exact entity bonuses into a single score. The user previously liked the hybrid method, so I should think of it as one cohesive strategy that includes BM25, exact identifiers, phrase boosts, and Gemini reranking of the top N results. This way, I can avoid separate lanes and RRF. If I integrate LLM, hybrid retrieval could be streamlined into one pipeline, which sounds more efficient. I’ll also think about a smaller schema with just essential tables.

payload.text:
**Simplifying the plan**

I think I need to rewrite the entire file to make it easier to understand while maintaining the essential standards and mentioning the current repository state. The user didn't ask for an exact wrapper right now, but editing the existing plan should be sufficient. I can use apply_patch to replace sections or the whole file, ensuring it's concise. 

Maybe it’s okay to have around 10-12 requirements and keep the phases P00-P03, including P04. Since there’s still no Cargo.toml, commands can be introduced later in P00 steps. I have to make sure the plan remains internally consistent.

**Designing a retrieval pipeline**

I need to ensure a direct approach that keeps both LLM and hybrid retrieval in mind. Let's outline the document sections required, using bullet points without bold text, and maintain the exact phase headers as `### Phase Pxx:`.

I'll define a single pipeline that includes ingesting `threads` metadata and rollout JSONL, normalizing into `sessions` and `chunks` (where a chunk is one user turn plus the assistant's response). I want to extract optional exact `entities` during chunk building, considering storing them as a compact text column or a separate table. 

To simplify, I prefer an `entity_text` column in the chunks table with a minimal schema that includes dialogue text and relevant metadata while indexing with FTS for efficient lookups.

**Structuring the search and retrieval**

I plan to use MATCH on the text and compute exact bonuses from `entity_text`, which seems like a good approach. 

For query planning, one Gemini prompt should return JSON with components like `exact_phrases`, `required_terms`, `optional_terms`, `excluded_terms`, and a `latest_bias` boolean. This allows it to be corpus-aware by passing top observed terms or entities from the filtered corpus. 

In the retrieval phase, I’ll set up one SQL retrieval step against `fts_chunks`, using exact bonuses and recency. I could mention “hybrid retrieval” to highlight the combination of full-text and exact entity/path bonuses.

**Evaluating test specifications**

I've got a plan for testing with standard sections. I’m considering six tests, like checking if bootstrap CLI works with `search` and `latest-spec`, and ingesting session metadata. I also want to review dialogue chunking and entity extraction. Another focus is ensuring the query planner is constrained to observed terms. Should I simplify by maybe dropping incremental indexing? The user prefers a smaller codebase, even though they previously asked for full testing. I can probably reduce the scope.

**Refining requirements**

I’m thinking it's good to clarify my requirements, which might total around ten. They include ingesting local Codex sources and filtering by repo/timeframe. I'll need to normalize the data into dialogue-first sessions with entity text. A single Gemini planner and a hybrid retrieval step using SQLite FTS sound useful too. Also, reranking top chunks and citing evidence verbatim are essential. I should keep the CLI surface minimal while ensuring local-first and deterministic tests, along with addressing any data privacy concerns.

**Planning hybrid retrieval tests**

I’m thinking about a hybrid approach: combining BM25 for dialogue, exact boosts on `entity_text` and phrases, plus a Gemini rerank. This strategy blends lexical full text with structured exact identifiers and LLM reranking, creating a straightforward path. I need to preserve the current session ID for regression testing and update the definitions. I’m outlining seven tests for current development, ensuring everything includes traceability and execution phases with red-green-refactor-measure methodology.

**Outlining phases for processing**

I'm breaking things down into four phases for the project. The first phase, P00, involves bootstrapping the CLI and setting config defaults. Next, in P01, I’ll focus on ingesting and chunking the data. Phase P02 will cover indexing, planning, and executing the search pipeline. Lastly, in P03, I’ll wrap up with the output, evaluation, and regression testing for the current session. This structure should help keep everything organized!

**Considering file updates**

I'm thinking I might use a here-doc with cat for easier file updates. I know that manual edits are forbidden, so I should probably use `apply_patch` to update the whole file. Since it's a huge file, it could be simplest to first delete everything and then add it back in with the delete and add commands. I need to ensure that the content remains exact. Let’s go ahead with that!

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Delete File: /home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md
*** Add File: /home/kirill/p/ask-codex-sessions/plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md
+=== document: plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md ===
+1. Title and Metadata
+
+- Project name: Ask Codex Session Search CLI
+- Version: 0.2.0-plan
+- Owners: Repository maintainer; implementation owner to be assigned in repo
+- Date: 2026-03-06
+- Document ID: PLAN-ASKCODEX-2026-03-06-002
+- Summary:
+  - Build a local-first Rust CLI that searches prior Codex CLI sessions stored on the same workstation and returns cited verbatim evidence for natural-language questions. The repository currently contains no Rust application code, no Cargo project, and no test runner; the implementation will add a small Rust CLI under `src/` and `tests/` while reading source data from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`. The plan uses one direct retrieval pipeline: filter the local corpus, ask Gemini to build a structured search plan from observed corpus terms, run one SQLite hybrid search over chunk text plus exact entity text, rerank the top candidates with Gemini, and return cited snippets.
+
+2. Design Consensus & Trade-offs
+
+- Topic: Implementation language
+  - Verdict: FOR Rust
+  - Rationale: The tool is a local CLI with parsing, SQLite, subprocess execution, and test-heavy development; Rust fits this without adding C++ complexity.
+- Topic: Retrieval architecture
+  - Verdict: FOR one integrated pipeline
+  - Rationale: The system should not have separate fast and robust modes, legacy paths, or fallbacks. It should always run the same steps in the same order.
+- Topic: LLM usage
+  - Verdict: FOR one Gemini planner and one Gemini rerank step
+  - Rationale: Gemini is useful for turning a natural-language question into a structured search plan and for checking the top retrieved chunks, but the codebase should not include multiple planners or a second exhaustive retrieval mode.
+- Topic: Hybrid retrieval
+  - Verdict: FOR SQLite FTS5 plus exact-entity bonuses plus Gemini rerank
+  - Rationale: Exact identifiers, paths, and phrases matter in Codex sessions; BM25 alone is not enough, but a compact hybrid score is simpler than multiple retrieval subsystems.
+- Topic: Storage
+  - Verdict: FOR one small SQLite index
+  - Rationale: Keep one local index file with only the data needed for search and citations. Avoid extra telemetry tables, vocab tables, and adapter layers until proven necessary.
+- Topic: Data model
+  - Verdict: FOR minimal schema
+  - Rationale: Store only `sessions`, `chunks`, and one FTS table. Keep entity extraction as chunk-level text instead of a separate graph-like structure.
+- Topic: CLI surface
+  - Verdict: FOR a minimal command surface
+  - Rationale: The first version should expose one default `search` command and one `latest-spec` preset that only changes query biasing, not architecture.
+- Topic: Depth and payloads
+  - Verdict: FOR dialogue-first v1
+  - Rationale: Default retrieval should use user and assistant dialogue plus exact entities derived from visible text. Tool payloads, edits, and reasoning stay out of v1 scope.
+- Topic: Testing strategy
+  - Verdict: FOR a small but strict gold-test harness
+  - Rationale: The current conversation session `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` is a concrete regression target. A small set of deterministic tests is enough for the first version.
+
+3. PRD (IEEE 29148 Stakeholder/System Needs)
+
+- Problem
+  - Prior Codex CLI sessions contain design history, interface decisions, exact identifiers, and implementation rationale that are hard to recover manually.
+  - The data already exists locally but is split across a SQLite metadata store and rollout JSONL files.
+- Users
+  - Primary user: a developer using Codex CLI who wants to recover previous design discussions and the latest spec for a topic.
+  - Secondary user: an AI assistant running in the same repository that needs reliable local retrieval with citations.
+- Value
+  - Recover previous decisions quickly.
+  - Preserve exact wording, identifiers, and file references.
+  - Reduce duplicated design work.
+- Business Goals
+  - Deliver a useful local CLI with small code size.
+  - Keep the architecture single-path and easy to maintain.
+  - Ground answers in verbatim evidence.
+- Success Metrics
+  - Current-session regression Top-1 exact thread hit = 1.00.
+  - Gold-query Recall@5 >= 0.85 on a small curated fixture set.
+  - Citation completeness = 100% for returned findings.
+  - Search latency p95 <= 5.0 s on a local corpus up to 1,000 threads.
+- Scope
+  - Local CLI only.
+  - Dialogue-first retrieval.
+  - Gemini-based query planning and reranking.
+  - SQLite FTS5-based hybrid retrieval.
+  - Citation and handoff output.
+- Non-goals
+  - Web UI.
+  - Dense vector retrieval.
+  - Multiple retrieval modes.
+  - Separate exhaustive scan mode.
+  - Tool/edit/reasoning depth modes in v1.
+  - Cloud services beyond the local Gemini CLI.
+- Dependencies
+  - Rust stable toolchain.
+  - Gemini CLI installed and authenticated locally.
+  - Local Codex sources present under `/home/kirill/.codex/`.
+  - SQLite with FTS5 support through Rust dependencies.
+- Risks
+  - Codex rollout format may vary slightly between sessions.
+  - Gemini JSON output may drift if prompts are too loose.
+  - Repeated system text may pollute rankings if chunking is naive.
+- Assumptions
+  - The repository is the implementation workspace and currently has no Rust code.
+  - The current-session rollout file remains available for regression testing.
+  - The user prefers one direct implementation path over fallback-heavy designs.
+
+4. SRS (IEEE 29148 Canonical Requirements)
+
+4.1 Functional Requirements
+
+- REQ-001, type: func
+  - The system shall ingest Codex thread metadata from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and rollout files from `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`.
+- REQ-002, type: func
+  - The system shall filter the local corpus by repo/cwd and timeframe before retrieval.
+- REQ-003, type: func
+  - The system shall normalize rollout files into dialogue chunks composed of one user turn plus the following assistant response text.
+- REQ-004, type: func
+  - The system shall derive exact entity text from visible session content, including file paths, identifiers, commands, and quoted phrases, and store that text with each chunk.
+- REQ-005, type: func
+  - The system shall ask Gemini for one structured query plan built from the user query and the observed terms in the filtered corpus.
+- REQ-006, type: func
+  - The system shall run one hybrid SQLite retrieval step that combines FTS5/BM25 over dialogue text with exact-match bonuses from phrase and entity text plus a recency bonus.
+- REQ-007, type: func
+  - The system shall rerank the top retrieved chunks with Gemini using the original query and chunk text.
+- REQ-008, type: func
+  - The system shall return verbatim cited evidence containing thread ID, date, rollout path, and source snippet.
+- REQ-009, type: func
+  - The system shall expose a minimal CLI with `search` and `latest-spec` entry points.
+- REQ-010, type: func
+  - The system shall emit a paste-ready handoff block for use in an active AI session.
+
+4.2 Non-functional Requirements
+
+- REQ-011, type: nfr
+  - The system shall remain local-first and shall not require a remote database or search service.
+- REQ-012, type: perf
+  - The system shall keep the implementation small by using one retrieval pipeline, one index, and one output format.
+- REQ-013, type: nfr
+  - The system shall support deterministic tests through fixed fixtures, mocked Gemini responses, and reproducible commands.
+
+4.3 Interfaces/APIs
+
+- REQ-014, type: int
+  - The CLI contract shall support:
+    - `ask-codex-sessions search "<query>"`
+    - `ask-codex-sessions latest-spec "<query>"`
+  - Planned core Rust types:
+    - `ThreadMeta`
+    - `Chunk`
+    - `QueryPlan`
+    - `SearchResult`
+    - `Citation`
+
+4.4 Data Requirements
+
+- REQ-015, type: data
+  - The system shall maintain a small local index with:
+    - one `sessions` table
+    - one `chunks` table
+    - one `fts_chunks` virtual table
+  - The system shall preserve exact values for session IDs, paths, identifiers, and quoted evidence.
+  - Dialogue text shall exclude raw tool payloads and reasoning text in v1.
+
+4.5 Error & Telemetry Expectations
+
+- REQ-016, type: func
+  - The system shall emit structured errors for missing local sources, malformed rollout files, invalid Gemini JSON, and missing citation spans.
+
+4.6 Acceptance Criteria
+
+- A query about the current discussion returns thread `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` with the correct rollout path and verbatim evidence.
+- `search` and `latest-spec` use the same implementation pipeline.
+- Gemini query expansion terms are constrained to observed corpus terms plus the original query text.
+- Output includes citations for every finding.
+- The v1 implementation does not require separate retrieval modes or fallbacks.
+
+4.7 System Architecture Diagram
+
+```mermaid
+flowchart LR
+  U[User Query] --> C[CLI]
+  C --> F[Repo and Time Filter]
+  F --> S[Observed Term Summary]
+  S --> P[Gemini Query Planner]
+  F --> I[(SQLite Index)]
+  P --> H[Hybrid SQLite Search]
+  I --> H
+  H --> R[Gemini Rerank Top K]
+  R --> O[Citation and Handoff Output]
+```
+
+```
+[Developer]
+    |
+    v
+[Rust CLI]
+    |
+    +--> [Source Reader]
+    |       - state_5.sqlite
+    |       - sessions/*.jsonl
+    |
+    +--> [Normalizer]
+    |       - ThreadMeta
+    |       - Chunk
+    |
+    +--> [SQLite Index]
+    |       - sessions
+    |       - chunks
+    |       - fts_chunks
+    |
+    +--> [Gemini Query Planner]
+    |
+    +--> [Hybrid Search]
+    |       - BM25 on dialogue_text
+    |       - exact phrase bonus
+    |       - exact entity bonus
+    |       - recency bonus
+    |
+    +--> [Gemini Rerank]
+    |
+    +--> [Citation Output]
+```
+
+5. Iterative Implementation & Test Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 + 29119-3)
+
+- Phase Strategy
+  - Build the smallest working CLI first.
+  - Add ingestion and indexing second.
+  - Add the single Gemini + hybrid retrieval pipeline third.
+  - Add output polish and gold regressions last.
+- Compute Policy
+  - branch_limits: 1 active implementation branch per phase.
+  - reflection_passes: 1 after the first green result in each phase.
+  - early_stop%: 95%; stop phase expansion once the phase exit gate is green.
+- Governance
+  - Any metric threshold change requires a new ADR entry.
+- State Safety
+  - Create a git tag before each phase transition: `plan-p00-green`, `plan-p01-green`, `plan-p02-green`, `plan-p03-green`.
+- Risk Register
+  - Risk: rollout parsing misses relevant text
+    - Trigger: chunk tests fail or citations are incomplete
+    - Mitigation: keep chunking simple and fixture-driven
+  - Risk: Gemini planner emits unusable terms
+    - Trigger: observed-term containment test fails
+    - Mitigation: use a strict JSON prompt and reject terms not seen in the filtered corpus
+  - Risk: retrieval overfits to repeated boilerplate
+    - Trigger: current-session regression ranks generic chunks above specific chunks
+    - Mitigation: strip obvious boilerplate during normalization and keep exact-entity bonuses
+- Suspension/Resumption Criteria
+  - Suspend if TEST-007 fails after P02 because the core search path is not trustworthy.
+  - Resume only after a deterministic fixture explains the failure.
+
+### Phase P00: Rust Bootstrap
+
+A. Scope and Objectives
+
+- Impacted REQ-009, REQ-011, REQ-013, REQ-014
+- Establish the Rust workspace, CLI entry points, and fixture/test runner.
+
+B. Iterative Execution Steps
+
+- Step 1 (RED): Create/update TEST-001 in `tests/cli_contract.rs` for REQ-009 and REQ-014 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because no Cargo project or CLI exists.
+- Step 2 (GREEN): Implement minimal `Cargo.toml`, `src/main.rs`, and `src/cli.rs` with `search` and `latest-spec` commands -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
+- Step 3 (RED): Create/update TEST-002 in `tests/config_defaults.rs` for REQ-011 and REQ-013 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-002 cargo test test_config_uses_local_codex_defaults --test config_defaults -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because local source defaults are absent.
+- Step 4 (GREEN): Implement `src/config.rs` with the local Codex source defaults -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-002 cargo test test_config_uses_local_codex_defaults --test config_defaults -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
+- Step 5 (REFACTOR): Move shared CLI/config types into `src/types.rs` -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
+- Step 6 (MEASURE): Run bootstrap evaluation -> run `EVAL_ID=EVAL-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: thresholds met.
+
+C. Exit Gate Rules
+
+- Green: CLI compiles, exposes two commands, and resolves local Codex defaults.
+- Yellow: CLI compiles but command contract is unstable.
+- Red: no test runner or no local path defaults.
+
+D. Phase Metrics
+
+- Confidence %: 95; standard Rust setup.
+- Long-term robustness %: 90; small CLI surface is stable.
+- Internal interactions: 4; CLI, config, types, fixtures.
+- External interactions: 0.
+- Complexity %: 20; bounded bootstrap work.
+- Feature creep %: 5; low if commands stay minimal.
+- Technical debt %: 8; low with early shared types.
+- YAGNI score: 93; everything introduced is immediately needed.
+- MoSCoW: Must.
+- Local/Non-local scope: Local.
+- Architectural changes count: 3.
+
+### Phase P01: Ingest and Index
+
+A. Scope and Objectives
+
+- Impacted REQ-001, REQ-002, REQ-003, REQ-004, REQ-015
+- Read local sources, build dialogue chunks, derive entity text, and store the small SQLite index.
+
+B. Iterative Execution Steps
+
+- Step 1 (RED): Create/update TEST-003 in `tests/source_ingest.rs` for REQ-001 and REQ-002 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-003 cargo test test_ingest_thread_metadata_from_fixture_sqlite --test source_ingest -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because source ingestion is absent.
+- Step 2 (GREEN): Implement `src/source.rs` for metadata reads and repo/time filters -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-003 cargo test test_ingest_thread_metadata_from_fixture_sqlite --test source_ingest -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
+- Step 3 (RED): Create/update TEST-004 in `tests/rollout_chunks.rs` for REQ-003 and REQ-004 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-004 cargo test test_rollout_becomes_dialogue_chunks_with_entity_text --test rollout_chunks -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because rollout parsing and entity extraction are absent.

  - Build a local-first Rust CLI that searches prior Codex CLI sessions stored on the same workstation and returns cited verbatim evidence for natural-language questions. The repository currently contains no Rust application code, no Cargo project, and no test runner; the implementation will add a small Rust CLI under `src/` and `tests/` while reading source data from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`. The plan uses one direct retrieval pipeline: filter the local corpus, ask Gemini to build a structured search plan from observed corpus terms, run one SQLite hybrid search over chunk text plus exact entity text, rerank the top candidates with Gemini, and return cited snippets.

2. Design Consensus & Trade-offs

- Topic: Implementation language
  - Verdict: FOR Rust
  - Rationale: The tool is a local CLI with parsing, SQLite, subprocess execution, and test-heavy development; Rust fits this without adding C++ complexity.
- Topic: Retrieval architecture
  - Verdict: FOR one integrated pipeline
  - Rationale: The system should not have separate fast and robust modes, legacy paths, or fallbacks. It should always run the same steps in the same order.
- Topic: LLM usage
  - Verdict: FOR one Gemini planner and one Gemini rerank step
  - Rationale: Gemini is useful for turning a natural-language question into a structured search plan and for checking the top retrieved chunks, but the codebase should not include multiple planners or a second exhaustive retrieval mode.
- Topic: Hybrid retrieval
  - Verdict: FOR SQLite FTS5 plus exact-entity bonuses plus Gemini rerank
  - Rationale: Exact identifiers, paths, and phrases matter in Codex sessions; BM25 alone is not enough, but a compact hybrid score is simpler than multiple retrieval subsystems.
- Topic: Storage
  - Verdict: FOR one small SQLite index
  - Rationale: Keep one local index file with only the data needed for search and citations. Avoid extra telemetry tables, vocab tables, and adapter layers until proven necessary.
- Topic: Data model
  - Verdict: FOR minimal schema
  - Rationale: Store only `sessions`, `chunks`, and one FTS table. Keep entity extraction as chunk-level text instead of a separate graph-like structure.
- Topic: CLI surface
  - Verdict: FOR a minimal command surface
  - Rationale: The first version should expose one default `search` command and one `latest-spec` preset that only changes query biasing, not architecture.
- Topic: Depth and payloads
  - Verdict: FOR dialogue-first v1
  - Rationale: Default retrieval should use user and assistant dialogue plus exact entities derived from visible text. Tool payloads, edits, and reasoning stay out of v1 scope.
- Topic: Testing strategy
  - Verdict: FOR a small but strict gold-test harness
  - Rationale: The current conversation session `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` is a concrete regression target. A small set of deterministic tests is enough for the first version.

3. PRD (IEEE 29148 Stakeholder/System Needs)

- Problem
  - Prior Codex CLI sessions contain design history, interface decisions, exact identifiers, and implementation rationale that are hard to recover manually.
  - The data already exists locally but is split across a SQLite metadata store and rollout JSONL files.
- Users
  - Primary user: a developer using Codex CLI who wants to recover previous design discussions and the latest spec for a topic.
  - Secondary user: an AI assistant running in the same repository that needs reliable local retrieval with citations.
- Value
  - Recover previous decisions quickly.
  - Preserve exact wording, identifiers, and file references.
  - Reduce duplicated design work.
- Business Goals
  - Deliver a useful local CLI with small code size.
  - Keep the architecture single-path and easy to maintain.
  - Ground answers in verbatim evidence.
- Success Metrics
  - Current-session regression Top-1 exact thread hit = 1.00.
  - Gold-query Recall@5 >= 0.85 on a small curated fixture set.
  - Citation completeness = 100% for returned findings.
  - Search latency p95 <= 5.0 s on a local corpus up to 1,000 threads.
- Scope
  - Local CLI only.
  - Dialogue-first retrieval.
  - Gemini-based query planning and reranking.
  - SQLite FTS5-based hybrid retrieval.
  - Citation and handoff output.
- Non-goals
  - Web UI.
  - Dense vector retrieval.
  - Multiple retrieval modes.
  - Separate exhaustive scan mode.
  - Tool/edit/reasoning depth modes in v1.
  - Cloud services beyond the local Gemini CLI.
- Dependencies
  - Rust stable toolchain.
  - Gemini CLI installed and authenticated locally.
  - Local Codex sources present under `/home/kirill/.codex/`.
  - SQLite with FTS5 support through Rust dependencies.
- Risks
  - Codex rollout format may vary slightly between sessions.
  - Gemini JSON output may drift if prompts are too loose.
  - Repeated system text may pollute rankings if chunking is naive.
- Assumptions
  - The repository is the implementation workspace and currently has no Rust code.
  - The current-session rollout file remains available for regression testing.
  - The user prefers one direct implementation path over fallback-heavy designs.

4. SRS (IEEE 29148 Canonical Requirements)

4.1 Functional Requirements

- REQ-001, type: func
  - The system shall ingest Codex thread metadata from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and rollout files from `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`.
- REQ-002, type: func
  - The system shall filter the local corpus by repo/cwd and timeframe before retrieval.
- REQ-003, type: func
  - The system shall normalize rollout files into dialogue chunks composed of one user turn plus the following assistant response text.
- REQ-004, type: func
  - The system shall derive exact entity text from visible session content, including file paths, identifiers, commands, and quoted phrases, and store that text with each chunk.
- REQ-005, type: func
  - The system shall ask Gemini for one structured query plan built from the user query and the observed terms in the filtered corpus.
- REQ-006, type: func
  - The system shall run one hybrid SQLite retrieval step that combines FTS5/BM25 over dialogue text with exact-match bonuses from phrase and entity text plus a recency bonus.
- REQ-007, type: func
  - The system shall rerank the top retrieved chunks with Gemini using the original query and chunk text.
- REQ-008, type: func
  - The system shall return verbatim cited evidence containing thread ID, date, rollout path, and source snippet.
- REQ-009, type: func
  - The system shall expose a minimal CLI with `search` and `latest-spec` entry points.
- REQ-010, type: func
  - The system shall emit a paste-ready handoff block for use in an active AI session.

4.2 Non-functional Requirements

- REQ-011, type: nfr
  - The system shall remain local-first and shall not require a remote database or search service.
- REQ-012, type: perf
  - The system shall keep the implementation small by using one retrieval pipeline, one index, and one output format.
- REQ-013, type: nfr
  - The system shall support deterministic tests through fixed fixtures, mocked Gemini responses, and reproducible commands.

4.3 Interfaces/APIs

- REQ-014, type: int
  - The CLI contract shall support:
    - `ask-codex-sessions search "<query>"`
    - `ask-codex-sessions latest-spec "<query>"`
  - Planned core Rust types:
    - `ThreadMeta`
    - `Chunk`
    - `QueryPlan`
    - `SearchResult`
    - `Citation`

4.4 Data Requirements

- REQ-015, type: data
  - The system shall maintain a small local index with:
    - one `sessions` table
    - one `chunks` table
    - one `fts_chunks` virtual table
  - The system shall preserve exact values for session IDs, paths, identifiers, and quoted evidence.
  - Dialogue text shall exclude raw tool payloads and reasoning text in v1.

4.5 Error & Telemetry Expectations

- REQ-016, type: func
  - The system shall emit structured errors for missing local sources, malformed rollout files, invalid Gemini JSON, and missing citation spans.

4.6 Acceptance Criteria

- A query about the current discussion returns thread `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` with the correct rollout path and verbatim evidence.
- `search` and `latest-spec` use the same implementation pipeline.
- Gemini query expansion terms are constrained to observed corpus terms plus the original query text.
- Output includes citations for every finding.
- The v1 implementation does not require separate retrieval modes or fallbacks.

4.7 System Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  U[User Query] --> C[CLI]
  C --> F[Repo and Time Filter]
  F --> S[Observed Term Summary]
  S --> P[Gemini Query Planner]
  F --> I[(SQLite Index)]
  P --> H[Hybrid SQLite Search]
  I --> H
  H --> R[Gemini Rerank Top K]
  R --> O[Citation and Handoff Output]
```

```
[Developer]
    |
    v
[Rust CLI]
    |
    +--> [Source Reader]
    |       - state_5.sqlite
    |       - sessions/*.jsonl
    |
    +--> [Normalizer]
    |       - ThreadMeta
    |       - Chunk
    |
    +--> [SQLite Index]
    |       - sessions
    |       - chunks
    |       - fts_chunks
    |
    +--> [Gemini Query Planner]
    |
    +--> [Hybrid Search]
    |       - BM25 on dialogue_text
    |       - exact phrase bonus
    |       - exact entity bonus
    |       - recency bonus
    |
    +--> [Gemini Rerank]
    |
    +--> [Citation Output]
```

5. Iterative Implementation & Test Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 + 29119-3)

- Phase Strategy
  - Build the smallest working CLI first.
  - Add ingestion and indexing second.
  - Add the single Gemini + hybrid retrieval pipeline third.
  - Add output polish and gold regressions last.
- Compute Policy
  - branch_limits: 1 active implementation branch per phase.
  - reflection_passes: 1 after the first green result in each phase.
  - early_stop%: 95%; stop phase expansion once the phase exit gate is green.
- Governance
  - Any metric threshold change requires a new ADR entry.
- State Safety
  - Create a git tag before each phase transition: `plan-p00-green`, `plan-p01-green`, `plan-p02-green`, `plan-p03-green`.
- Risk Register
  - Risk: rollout parsing misses relevant text
    - Trigger: chunk tests fail or citations are incomplete
    - Mitigation: keep chunking simple and fixture-driven
  - Risk: Gemini planner emits unusable terms
    - Trigger: observed-term containment test fails
    - Mitigation: use a strict JSON prompt and reject terms not seen in the filtered corpus
  - Risk: retrieval overfits to repeated boilerplate
    - Trigger: current-session regression ranks generic chunks above specific chunks
    - Mitigation: strip obvious boilerplate during normalization and keep exact-entity bonuses
- Suspension/Resumption Criteria
  - Suspend if TEST-007 fails after P02 because the core search path is not trustworthy.
  - Resume only after a deterministic fixture explains the failure.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

=== document: plans/ask-codex-session-search-plan.md ===
1. Title and Metadata

- Project name: Ask Codex Session Search CLI
- Version: 0.2.0-plan
- Owners: Repository maintainer; implementation owner to be assigned in repo
- Date: 2026-03-06
- Document ID: PLAN-ASKCODEX-2026-03-06-002
- Summary:
  - Build a local-first Rust CLI that searches prior Codex CLI sessions stored on the same workstation and returns cited verbatim evidence for natural-language questions. The repository currently contains no Rust application code, no Cargo project, and no test runner; the implementation will add a small Rust CLI under `src/` and `tests/` while reading source data from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`. The plan uses one direct retrieval pipeline: filter the local corpus, ask Gemini to build a structured search plan from observed corpus terms, run one SQLite hybrid search over chunk text plus exact entity text, rerank the top candidates with Gemini, and return cited snippets.

2. Design Consensus & Trade-offs

- Topic: Implementation language
  - Verdict: FOR Rust
  - Rationale: The tool is a local CLI with parsing, SQLite, subprocess execution, and test-heavy development; Rust fits this without adding C++ complexity.
- Topic: Retrieval architecture
  - Verdict: FOR one integrated pipeline
  - Rationale: The system should not have separate fast and robust modes, legacy paths, or fallbacks. It should always run the same steps in the same order.
- Topic: LLM usage
  - Verdict: FOR one Gemini planner and one Gemini rerank step
  - Rationale: Gemini is useful for turning a natural-language question into a structured search plan and for checking the top retrieved chunks, but the codebase should not include multiple planners or a second exhaustive retrieval mode.
- Topic: Hybrid retrieval
  - Verdict: FOR SQLite FTS5 plus exact-entity bonuses plus Gemini rerank
  - Rationale: Exact identifiers, paths, and phrases matter in Codex sessions; BM25 alone is not enough, but a compact hybrid score is simpler than multiple retrieval subsystems.
- Topic: Storage
  - Verdict: FOR one small SQLite index
  - Rationale: Keep one local index file with only the data needed for search and citations. Avoid extra telemetry tables, vocab tables, and adapter layers until proven necessary.
- Topic: Data model
  - Verdict: FOR minimal schema
  - Rationale: Store only `sessions`, `chunks`, and one FTS table. Keep entity extraction as chunk-level text instead of a separate graph-like structure.
- Topic: CLI surface
  - Verdict: FOR a minimal command surface
  - Rationale: The first version should expose one default `search` command and one `latest-spec` preset that only changes query biasing, not architecture.
- Topic: Depth and payloads
  - Verdict: FOR dialogue-first v1
  - Rationale: Default retrieval should use user and assistant dialogue plus exact entities derived from visible text. Tool payloads, edits, and reasoning stay out of v1 scope.
- Topic: Testing strategy
  - Verdict: FOR a small but strict gold-test harness
  - Rationale: The current conversation session `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` is a concrete regression target. A small set of deterministic tests is enough for the first version.

3. PRD (IEEE 29148 Stakeholder/System Needs)

- Problem
  - Prior Codex CLI sessions contain design history, interface decisions, exact identifiers, and implementation rationale that are hard to recover manually.
  - The data already exists locally but is split across a SQLite metadata store and rollout JSONL files.
- Users
  - Primary user: a developer using Codex CLI who wants to recover previous design discussions and the latest spec for a topic.
  - Secondary user: an AI assistant running in the same repository that needs reliable local retrieval with citations.
- Value
  - Recover previous decisions quickly.
  - Preserve exact wording, identifiers, and file references.
  - Reduce duplicated design work.
- Business Goals
  - Deliver a useful local CLI with small code size.
  - Keep the architecture single-path and easy to maintain.
  - Ground answers in verbatim evidence.
- Success Metrics
  - Current-session regression Top-1 exact thread hit = 1.00.
  - Gold-query Recall@5 >= 0.85 on a small curated fixture set.
  - Citation completeness = 100% for returned findings.
  - Search latency p95 <= 5.0 s on a local corpus up to 1,000 threads.
- Scope
  - Local CLI only.
  - Dialogue-first retrieval.
  - Gemini-based query planning and reranking.
  - SQLite FTS5-based hybrid retrieval.
  - Citation and handoff output.
- Non-goals
  - Web UI.
  - Dense vector retrieval.
  - Multiple retrieval modes.
  - Separate exhaustive scan mode.
  - Tool/edit/reasoning depth modes in v1.
  - Cloud services beyond the local Gemini CLI.
- Dependencies
  - Rust stable toolchain.
  - Gemini CLI installed and authenticated locally.
  - Local Codex sources present under `/home/kirill/.codex/`.
  - SQLite with FTS5 support through Rust dependencies.
- Risks
  - Codex rollout format may vary slightly between sessions.
  - Gemini JSON output may drift if prompts are too loose.
  - Repeated system text may pollute rankings if chunking is naive.
- Assumptions
  - The repository is the implementation workspace and currently has no Rust code.
  - The current-session rollout file remains available for regression testing.
  - The user prefers one direct implementation path over fallback-heavy designs.

4. SRS (IEEE 29148 Canonical Requirements)

4.1 Functional Requirements

- REQ-001, type: func
  - The system shall ingest Codex thread metadata from `/home/kirill/.codex/state_5.sqlite` and rollout files from `/home/kirill/.codex/sessions/`.
- REQ-002, type: func
  - The system shall filter the local corpus by repo/cwd and timeframe before retrieval.
- REQ-003, type: func
  - The system shall normalize rollout files into dialogue chunks composed of one user turn plus the following assistant response text.
- REQ-004, type: func
  - The system shall derive exact entity text from visible session content, including file paths, identifiers, commands, and quoted phrases, and store that text with each chunk.
- REQ-005, type: func
  - The system shall ask Gemini for one structured query plan built from the user query and the observed terms in the filtered corpus.
- REQ-006, type: func
  - The system shall run one hybrid SQLite retrieval step that combines FTS5/BM25 over dialogue text with exact-match bonuses from phrase and entity text plus a recency bonus.
- REQ-007, type: func
  - The system shall rerank the top retrieved chunks with Gemini using the original query and chunk text.
- REQ-008, type: func
  - The system shall return verbatim cited evidence containing thread ID, date, rollout path, and source snippet.
- REQ-009, type: func
  - The system shall expose a minimal CLI with `search` and `latest-spec` entry points.
- REQ-010, type: func
  - The system shall emit a paste-ready handoff block for use in an active AI session.

4.2 Non-functional Requirements

- REQ-011, type: nfr
  - The system shall remain local-first and shall not require a remote database or search service.
- REQ-012, type: perf
  - The system shall keep the implementation small by using one retrieval pipeline, one index, and one output format.
- REQ-013, type: nfr
  - The system shall support deterministic tests through fixed fixtures, mocked Gemini responses, and reproducible commands.

4.3 Interfaces/APIs

- REQ-014, type: int
  - The CLI contract shall support:
    - `ask-codex-sessions search "<query>"`
    - `ask-codex-sessions latest-spec "<query>"`
  - Planned core Rust types:
    - `ThreadMeta`
    - `Chunk`
    - `QueryPlan`
    - `SearchResult`
    - `Citation`

4.4 Data Requirements

- REQ-015, type: data
  - The system shall maintain a small local index with:
    - one `sessions` table
    - one `chunks` table
    - one `fts_chunks` virtual table
  - The system shall preserve exact values for session IDs, paths, identifiers, and quoted evidence.
  - Dialogue text shall exclude raw tool payloads and reasoning text in v1.

4.5 Error & Telemetry Expectations

- REQ-016, type: func
  - The system shall emit structured errors for missing local sources, malformed rollout files, invalid Gemini JSON, and missing citation spans.

4.6 Acceptance Criteria

- A query about the current discussion returns thread `019cc49c-0918-7c11-9a8a-630c28b9b443` with the correct rollout path and verbatim evidence.
- `search` and `latest-spec` use the same implementation pipeline.
- Gemini query expansion terms are constrained to observed corpus terms plus the original query text.
- Output includes citations for every finding.
- The v1 implementation does not require separate retrieval modes or fallbacks.

4.7 System Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  U[User Query] --> C[CLI]
  C --> F[Repo and Time Filter]
  F --> S[Observed Term Summary]
  S --> P[Gemini Query Planner]
  F --> I[(SQLite Index)]
  P --> H[Hybrid SQLite Search]
  I --> H
  H --> R[Gemini Rerank Top K]
  R --> O[Citation and Handoff Output]
```

```
[Developer]
    |
    v
[Rust CLI]
    |
    +--> [Source Reader]
    |       - state_5.sqlite
    |       - sessions/*.jsonl
    |
    +--> [Normalizer]
    |       - ThreadMeta
    |       - Chunk
    |
    +--> [SQLite Index]
    |       - sessions
    |       - chunks
    |       - fts_chunks
    |
    +--> [Gemini Query Planner]
    |
    +--> [Hybrid Search]
    |       - BM25 on dialogue_text
    |       - exact phrase bonus
    |       - exact entity bonus
    |       - recency bonus
    |
    +--> [Gemini Rerank]
    |
    +--> [Citation Output]
```

5. Iterative Implementation & Test Plan (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 + 29119-3)

- Phase Strategy
  - Build the smallest working CLI first.
  - Add ingestion and indexing second.
  - Add the single Gemini + hybrid retrieval pipeline third.
  - Add output polish and gold regressions last.
- Compute Policy
  - branch_limits: 1 active implementation branch per phase.
  - reflection_passes: 1 after the first green result in each phase.
  - early_stop%: 95%; stop phase expansion once the phase exit gate is green.
- Governance
  - Any metric threshold change requires a new ADR entry.
- State Safety
  - Create a git tag before each phase transition: `plan-p00-green`, `plan-p01-green`, `plan-p02-green`, `plan-p03-green`.
- Risk Register
  - Risk: rollout parsing misses relevant text
    - Trigger: chunk tests fail or citations are incomplete
    - Mitigation: keep chunking simple and fixture-driven
  - Risk: Gemini planner emits unusable terms
    - Trigger: observed-term containment test fails
    - Mitigation: use a strict JSON prompt and reject terms not seen in the filtered corpus
  - Risk: retrieval overfits to repeated boilerplate
    - Trigger: current-session regression ranks generic chunks above specific chunks
    - Mitigation: strip obvious boilerplate during normalization and keep exact-entity bonuses
- Suspension/Resumption Criteria
  - Suspend if TEST-007 fails after P02 because the core search path is not trustworthy.
  - Resume only after a deterministic fixture explains the failure.

### Phase P00: Rust Bootstrap

A. Scope and Objectives

- Impacted REQ-009, REQ-011, REQ-013, REQ-014
- Establish the Rust workspace, CLI entry points, and fixture/test runner.

B. Iterative Execution Steps

- Step 1 (RED): Create/update TEST-001 in `tests/cli_contract.rs` for REQ-009 and REQ-014 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because no Cargo project or CLI exists.
- Step 2 (GREEN): Implement minimal `Cargo.toml`, `src/main.rs`, and `src/cli.rs` with `search` and `latest-spec` commands -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 3 (RED): Create/update TEST-002 in `tests/config_defaults.rs` for REQ-011 and REQ-013 -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-002 cargo test test_config_uses_local_codex_defaults --test config_defaults -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: FAIL because local source defaults are absent.
- Step 4 (GREEN): Implement `src/config.rs` with the local Codex source defaults -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-002 cargo test test_config_uses_local_codex_defaults --test config_defaults -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 5 (REFACTOR): Move shared CLI/config types into `src/types.rs` -> run `TEST_ID=TEST-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: PASS.
- Step 6 (MEASURE): Run bootstrap evaluation -> run `EVAL_ID=EVAL-001 cargo test test_cli_has_search_and_latest_spec --test cli_contract -- --exact --nocapture` -> expected: thresholds met.

C. Exit Gate Rules

- Green: CLI compiles, exposes two commands, and resolves local Codex defaults.
- Yellow: CLI compiles but command contract is unstable.
- Red: no test runner or no local path defaults.

D. Phase Metrics

- Confidence %: 95; standard Rust setup.
